title: SQL Lab 4: Latest Order per Customer
short_description: Pick one deterministic latest order per customer.
business_context: Customer support dashboards often need each customer's latest order. Ties must be deterministic so reruns do not flip results.
problem_statement: Return the latest order for each customer. If two orders have the same order_date, keep the larger order_id.
student_task: Write a query returning order_id, customer_id, order_date, and amount.
learning_goals: Use ROW_NUMBER for one surviving row
Avoid duplicate latest rows when dates tie
broken_code:
SELECT o.*
FROM orders o
JOIN (
  SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) AS max_order_date
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
) m
ON o.customer_id = m.customer_id
AND o.order_date = m.max_order_date;
production_logs: review-note: max-date join does not resolve same-date ties
solution_query:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT order_id,
         customer_id,
         order_date,
         amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY customer_id
           ORDER BY order_date DESC, order_id DESC
         ) AS rn
  FROM orders
)
SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1;
explanation: ROW_NUMBER keeps exactly one row per customer. Ordering by order_date descending and order_id descending makes the tie behavior deterministic.
common_mistakes: Returning all tied latest rows when one row is required
hints: One output row should represent one customer.
A max-date join can return duplicates.
